query: How do PostgreSQL and MySQL differ in default isolation level?
report:
# How do PostgreSQL and MySQL differ in default isolation level?

PostgreSQL defaults to the READ COMMITTED isolation level [1]. MySQL with the InnoDB engine defaults to REPEATABLE READ instead [2]. The practical difference is that MySQL's default prevents non-repeatable reads within a transaction, whereas PostgreSQL's does not [1].

corpus.text: PostgreSQL uses READ COMMITTED as the default transaction isolation level; non-repeatable reads are possible.
InnoDB uses REPEATABLE READ as its default isolation level, preventing non-repeatable reads within a transaction.

expected_behavior: names both defaults (PostgreSQL READ COMMITTED, MySQL REPEATABLE READ)
